name: aloe vera
intensity: No direct intensity data. Contextual inference: Laxative, wound healing, skin soother. Herbs often act via neurotransmitter modulation, antioxidant effects, or enzyme inhibition.. mechanism: Topical aloe gel contains polysaccharides and related constituents that may support hydration and local wound-healing processes. Oral laxative effects are primarily linked to anthraquinone constituents in aloe latex (e.g., barbaloin/aloin derivatives).
safetyNotes: Topical gel is usually well tolerated, but burning, itching, rash, or eczema can occur. Oral aloe latex can cause abdominal pain, cramping, and diarrhea. Oral aloe leaf extract has been associated with acute hepatitis in case reports, and non-decolorized whole-leaf extract has raised carcinogenicity concerns.

sources.title: NCCIH: Aloe Vera — Usefulness and Safety

description: Aloe vera products are used topically (especially gel) and orally (gel or latex-containing products). Evidence is stronger for selected topical uses than for most oral indications.
dosage: Product forms are not interchangeable. No universal evidence-based dose applies across topical gel, oral gel, and latex-containing products.
interactions: Oral aloe latex may increase adverse effects when combined with other stimulant laxatives.
Potential additive effects with glucose-lowering therapies if oral aloe products lower blood glucose.
legalStatus: Approved for external use; oral use regulated
preparation: Topical use typically uses inner-leaf gel. Oral products vary substantially; latex-containing or whole-leaf extracts have different safety profiles from decolorized inner-leaf gel products.
region: Native to the Arabian Peninsula and now cultivated widely in subtropical and tropical regions.
